The great advantage of using building blocks is that you can use them to create a customer layout and yet still benefit from the advantages of the metadata driven approach: low code, built in enterprise-grade capabilities such as multi-language support, accessibility, security and performance, and design system consistency.

Find out more in this six-part series of blog posts, here is the first one, which contains links to the others:

  • Introducing the SAP Fiori Development Portal: Your Gateway to Rapid SAP Fiori App Creation (1 of 6).

We have also restructured our documentation for SAP Fiori elements, with separate entry points for OData V4 and OData V2, and better supporting the building block approach. Read more here:

  • SAP Fiori Elements: New Documentation Structure.

Using AI to Develop UIs – MCP Servers, Joule

The above posts from Q1/2025 and Q3/2025 introduced a number of powerful ways to accelerate UI development by using AI, such as:

  • Creating SAP Fiori elements apps from a sketch (in the Q1 post), now integrated in SAP Build Code and Joule (Q3 post)
  • Joule for SAPUI5 freestyle development (Q1 post)
  • AI-assisted auto-fix of coding errors with UI5 linter (Q3 post)

Recently, we have gone even further and introduced MCP servers for freestyle SAPUI5 development as well as for developing with SAP Fiori elements and the associated building blocks. These open-source Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers provide AI agents with comprehensive UI5 and SAP Fiori elements knowledge, including best practices and project-aware context information, and thus provide developers with valuable support. Figure 3 shows examples.

Figure 3: Examples of the UI5 MCP Server (on the left) and the MCP Server for SAP Fiori (on the right) in action. Alt Text: both images show screenshots of a CLINE console view with a task entered as text at the top, followed by a list of steps which the system proposes to do to solve the task.

Read about these new MCP servers in these blog posts:

  • UI5 MCP Server.
  • MCP Server for SAP Fiori.
  • Setting the right expectations around AI for code generation and getting past the inherent limits

Alternatively, you can listen to these podcasts involving Tim Back and Florian Vogt, talking about the intricacies of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) within the SAP ecosystem:

  • Spotify: UI5 & MCP - Navigating the Model Context Protocol in SAP.
  • Apple Podcast: UI5 & MCP - Navigating the Model Context Protocol in SAP.

Latest UI5 MCP Server Enhancements

The UI5 MCP server got extended to help migrate existing JavaScript code to TypeScript, as well as for generating UI Integration Cards.

New TypeScript Migration Feature

With the newest feature of the UI5 MCP server, UI5-specific TypeScript conversion assistance is now available to AI coding agents, making the task much easier. Find out more in this blog post:

  • UI5 MCP Server's New TypeScript Migration Feature - Real-World Experience and Best Practices

Generating UI Integration Cards

The UI5 MCP Server now supports the development of UI Integration Cards. In its latest version – v0.2.1 – it includes three new tools dedicated to UI Integration Cards:

  1. Best practice guidance for your agent.
  2. Scaffolding a new Integration Card.
  3. Validating the Card’s manifest.

These tools assist Large Language Models (LLMs) in creating new cards from scratch or editing existing ones, following the best practices for card development. Find out more here:

  • UI5 MCP Server Got Extended for UI Integration Cards Generation.

AI News for SAP Fiori Tools and MCP Server for SAP Fiori

The Project Accelerator can now be launched directly from the SAP Fiori generator, streamlining the initial project creation flow. The previously required staging step has been removed, allowing new projects to be created directly in the selected workspace folder. For projects created using the Project Accelerator, the Application Modeler now offers advanced editing capabilities, such as creating new fields and properties end-to-end, updating the data model, exposing the service, and consuming the changes in the UI in one coherent flow.

AI support has also been extended to mock data generation. Developers can generate realistic mock data using AI directly in the Application Modeler, and this capability is available for all applications whenever the Project Accelerator is enabled.

In parallel, the MCP server for SAP Fiori continues to expand. The server now exposes @sap-ux/create commands as CLI functionality, enabling reliable execution in MCP-supported workflows. App generation has been extended to support CAP Java projects, and a new “search_docs” tool allows the LLM (Large Language Model) to query SAP Fiori elements documentation, annotations, SAP Fiori tools documentation, and SAP Fiori Development Portal resources to quickly find relevant guidance and code snippets.

Joule Enhancement: Create Freestyle SAPUI5 Apps in SAP Build Code

You can now use Joule in SAP Build Code to create new freestyle SAPUI5 apps according to your requirements. The feature is not absolutely groundbreaking, as there are other tools doing a similar job like the Easy-UI5 templates and the recently published UI5 MCP Server. But:

  1. It brings the functionality right to the fingertips of developers using SAP Build Code – no other tools needed.
  2. It extends the template capabilities beyond what the Easy-UI5 templates provide.
  3. Implementing it within the Build Code Joule environment yielded interesting insights and further improvements.

Find out more about the feature, and in the second “making of” post, read about how Andreas Kunz went about building it, and what he learnt from doing it:

  • Joule in SAP Build Code can now create freestyle UI5 apps.
  • Inside Joule: Lessons from building an AI coding assistant for SAP Build.

SAP Fiori Tools News

In addition to the AI-enhancements mentioned above, we now provide expanded support for SAP Fiori elements building blocks, a new connection manager for SAP Systems in VS Code, and a feature to preview SAP Fiori web apps on mobile devices.

Expanded Support for SAP Fiori Elements Building Blocks

The following enhancements make it easier to adapt applications and allow developers to work more confidently and efficiently with SAP Fiori elements–based UIs.

Developers can now add more building block types to their apps with the Page Editor, including

  • rich text editors within custom sections,
  • custom columns for table building blocks, and
  • the page building block itself.

Managing custom pages is also more intuitive, as building blocks can now be deleted directly from the editor.

To support experimentation and iteration, undo and redo actions are now available when working on custom pages.

New Connection Manager for SAP Systems in VS Code

We have introduced the Connection Manager for SAP Systems as a dedicated Visual Studio Code extension for managing connections to remote SAP systems. The Connection Manager allows developers to create, store, test, and edit connection information that can be reused by the SAP Fiori generator as well as by preview features in SAP Fiori tools.

Preview SAP Fiori Web Apps on Mobile

When developing SAP Fiori applications, simulating a mobile experience in a desktop browser often isn’t enough to fully validate real-world behavior. Touch interactions, performance characteristics, and device-specific rendering can differ noticeably on physical phones and tablets.

To address this, SAP Fiori tools now supports previewing applications directly on real mobile devices. You do this by using @sap/ux-ui5-tooling version 1.19.1 or higher and creating a new run configuration and enabling Remote Access. When you start the run configuration, the terminal provides connection details, including a URL and a QR code, which can be opened directly on a phone or tablet to access the running app.

Find out more here:

  • SAP Fiori tools 2601 update – Mobile Device Preview, Building Blocks, and AI-Driven Productivity.
  • SAP Fiori Development newsletter January 2026 (issue #38).
  • SAP Fiori Development newsletter issue #39 (March 2026).

 

News for Mobile Designers and Developers

Let’s first look at updates in the design guidelines and the SDKs, and then look at news for the mobile development kit (MDK).

Mobile Design Guideline and SDK Updates

We have introduced numerous new and enhanced patterns and components this last half-year. I have selected a small number to highlight here, further below you’ll find links to get the full overview.

New for AI

The following two new patterns have been introduced for both the SAP Fiori for Android and the SAP Fiori for iOS design guidelines.

AI Insights Pattern: We have introduced a new AI Insights Pattern, which describes how to present AI insights optimally on mobile devices, as shown in Figure 4. It provides a standardized way to display a summary, analysis, or suggestion generated with AI. This pattern can be applied to a full-screen or a card depending on the scope of content generated by AI.

AI Handoff Pattern: We have created a new guideline describing the AI Handoff Pattern, which defines how a user should transition between contextual embedded AI features and the Joule conversational panel.

Figure 4: Two new AI patterns: AI Insights Pattern and AI Handoff Pattern. Alt Text: On the left two image for AI Insights Pattern, first a full mobile phone screen with title and subtitle at the top, followed by two insights sections, each with two slots for content, at the bottom a user feedback row with thumbs up and thumbs down icons, above a an AI generate button. On the right a diagram for the AI Handoff Pattern showing a schematic mobile module with arrow to and from a schematic Joule UI.

Further news

I have picked two stand-out updates, also made available for Android and iOS.

New Illustrations: We have introduced the new illustrations, that were also introduced in the web, to the mobile design system and the mobile SDKs. You can see a few examples on the left in Figure 5 below.

More flexible card layouts: We offer far more flexibility now in how you use cards to summarize information. For example, you can create wider cards that have the image on the left or the right, and the content block next to it. With the new flex item component, the card’s main header can now be customized to hold more content by adding tags and status info labels outside, on top, between, or below the title and subtitle. Two examples are shown in Figure 5 on the right.

Figure 5: New illustrations and more flexible card layout and content options. Alt Text: On the left three different-size “My Daily Tasks” cards, with images for “You’r all caught up”, “Oops” and “Congratulations”. On the right two card examples: at the top a slim wide card with title, subtitle and one line text on the left, image on the right; below that a larger card with image on the left, title and details with colored indicators and buttons on the right.

Find out more here:

  • Introducing SAP Fiori for Android/iOS 25.8: Innovative Enhancements for Android and iOS.

For an overview of all updates, have a look at the what’s new sections in the SAP Design System portal and the SAP Mobile Services Documentation

  • SAP Fiori for Android design: What's New for SAP BTP SDK for Android 26.1 and older.
  • SAP BTP SDK for Android: What’s New in Release 26.1.2 and earlier releases.
  • SAP Fiori for iOS design: What's New for SAP BTP SDK for iOS 26.1 and older.
  • SAP BTP SDK for iOS: What’s New in Version 26.1.2 and earlier releases.

Mobile Development Kit (MDK) News

The Mobile Development Kit, available as part of SAP Mobile Services, enables developers and technical business users to build cross-platform mobile applications. It allows you to build your application once, in an integrated development environment (SAP Business Application Studio / VSCode extension) and run it natively on Mobile Android and iOS devices.  

Here I have chosen to highlight one area: enhancements to the calander control, which now supports date range selection as well as allowing you to highlight special events or statuses directly on the calendar.

The new date range selection displays the calendar in a month-view format and allows multiple months to be shown. Users can scroll vertically through the calendar months. This is ideal if you need to plan events or manage schedules over extended periods, since users can select a time period with a start date and an end date (see Figure 6 below):

  • The first date selected becomes the start date of the range.
  • The second date selected becomes the end date of the range.

Figure 6: Date range selection supported by the mobile development kit provided by SAP Mobile Service. Alt Text: An image of an iPhone and an Android phone next to each other. The iPhone with the light Morning Horizon theme and a date selection Sep 16 – Sep 29, 2025, the other with the dark Evening Horizon theme and a date selection Mon Sep 29 to Fri Oct 17 2025.

Find out more here:

  • Blog post: What’s new in Mobile development kit client 25.9.
  • Documentation: What’s New in Mobile Development Kit 25.9.1 and earlier releases.

 

Further Information

For creating web applications, do register for the upcoming UI5con virtual conference.

Learn more about it at https://openui5.org/ui5con/faq.html, including how to attend, sponsor, or propose a topic for a session.

For links to design and development tools and technology, have a look at the SAP Fiori topic page in the SAP Community, or go directly to these pages:

  • SAP Design System.
  • SAP Fiori tools.
  • SAPUI5.
  • SAP Fiori elements.
  • SAP Mobile technologies.

 

Final words

As you can see from all seven posts in the series, we continue to progress rapidly with the user experience of our product portfolio, especially with regards to AI-assistance.

Part 1 of the series lists all the parts, and links to those parts that have already been published:

  • SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

Do keep posting your experiences and recommendations yourself in the SAP Community, with the SAP Fiori and/or the User Experience tag! In case you are wondering how to get a list of the most recent blog posts on SAP Fiori and User Experience in our new SAP Community, use these links:

  • Latest blog posts on SAP Fiori in the SAP Community.
  • Latest blog posts on User Experience in the SAP Community.

For general information on design at SAP and SAP Fiori, check out:

  • www.sap.com/design.
  • www.sap.com/fiori.
  • SAP Community: SAP Fiori topic page.

entry.title: Step-by-Step Integration of SAP Cloud ALM with S/4HANA for Transport Management
entry.content:
This blog outlines the process of integrating SAP Cloud ALM with SAP S/4 HANA to enable efficient transport management. The integration ensures streamlined lifecycle management, improved visibility, and better control over transport activities across the SAP landscape.

The overall process involves the following key steps:

  1. Onboarding SAP Cloud ALM

Before initiating the integration, SAP Cloud ALM must be provisioned and onboarded in your SAP environment.

Prerequisites

Ensure the following requirement is met before starting the onboarding process:

You must have an S-user ID with the “Edit Cloud Data” authorization in SAP for Me.

Procedure

  • Log in to SAP for Me.
  • Navigate to Systems & Provisioning.
  • Under Products Available for Provisioning, locate your entitlement for SAP Cloud ALM.

  • Choose Start Provisioning.

Select the geographical region in which you would like to provision SAP Cloud ALM 

  • Enter a unique subdomain name.
  • Specify an SAP Cloud Identity Service, if you have an existing one please select it from the list, if you do not have an existing SAP Cloud Identity Service a new one will be created for you automatically.

  • After selecting Submit, the provisioning process is initiated. SAP Cloud ALM is automatically prepared by SAP, and the required foundational entities are created to enable the service.

During this process, the following components are provisioned:

  • A SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) Global Account and Subaccount dedicated to SAP Cloud ALM are created. Within this subaccount, you will generate the Service API key, which is required to connect SAP Cloud ALM with other systems in your landscape.
  • A mutual trust relationship is automatically established between SAP Cloud ALM and the selected SAP Cloud Identity Authentication Service tenant to enable secure authentication and user access.

Once SAP completes the setup of these entities, you will receive a confirmation email containing the URL to access your newly provisioned SAP Cloud ALM tenant.

  1. System Prerequisites

SAP_BASIS Release

Your ABAP system must meet one of the following requirements:

  • SAP_BASIS 7.40 SP16 or higher (or 7.50 SP05 or higher)
  • SAP_BASIS 7.40 SP09 – SP15 (or 7.50 SP00 – SP04) with implementation of SAP Note 2283880

SAP_UI Version

The minimum required version is:

  • SAP_UI 740 SP15 or higher

ST-PI Component

Ensure the ST-PI component is properly maintained:

  • Install ST-PI 7.40 support packages and keep them up to date.
  • Implement the required collective correction SAP Note 3639977, which includes updates for SAP Cloud ALM compatibility.
  • Always operate the ABAP system with the latest or second latest support package available on the SAP Support Portal.

ST-A/PI Version

For Business Process Monitoring in SAP S/4HANA or SAP Business Suite 7:

  • Use ST-A/PI version higher than 01U_731.
  • Keep the component updated regularly.
  • If using ST-A/PI version 01W, ensure that the latest related SAP Notes are implemented.

Security and SSL Configuration

The following profile parameters and certificates must be configured in the ABAP system:

Required Profile Parameters

  • icm/HTTPS/client_sni_enabled = TRUE
  • ssl/client_ciphersuites must be maintained according to SAP Note 510007

Required Root Certificates

The following certificates must be imported in STRUST under:

  • SSL Client (Anonymous)
  • SSL Client (Standard)

Certificates required:

  • DigiCert Global Root G2
  • DigiCert TLS RSA4096 Root G5

Required User Authorizations

The user who runs transaction /SDF/ALM_SETUP must be assigned the following PFCG role:

  • SAP_SDF_ALM_SETUP

Within this role, maintain the authorization object:

  • S_BTCH_NAM → BTCUNAME

The value for BTCUNAME can be maintained as:

  • * (asterisk) to allow any user to execute the background job, or
  • The specific username that will run the background job for data collection.

Background Job User designated to run the data collection background jobs must have the appropriate roles and authorizations required for SAP Cloud ALM data integration. 

Configure Transport Management System (STMS) in SAP S/4HANA

Log in to the SAP S/4HANA application server and configure the SAP Transport Management System (STMS) to enable transport management across your system landscape.

In this example, a two-system landscape is used:

  • Development System: NI2
  • Production System: PI2

The first step is to establish the STMS configuration between the NI2 (Development) system and the PI2 (Production) system. This configuration ensures that transport requests created in the development system can be properly routed and imported into the production system following the defined transport path.

Make sure CTC parameter value maintained in DEV and prod system as 1

  1. Connecting the On-Premise System to SAP Cloud ALM

Once the SAP Transport Management System (STMS) configuration is completed between the systems, the next step is to integrate the on-premise SAP S/4HANA system with SAP Cloud ALM.

This integration enables SAP Cloud ALM to monitor transport activities, manage deployment processes, and provide centralized visibility across the entire system landscape. 

Integration Setup Across System Landscapes

To establish the integration with SAP Cloud ALM, the required configuration steps must be performed in both the Development (DEV) and Production (PROD) systems. In addition, the setup should be executed in client 000 as well as in the productive client of each system.

While the technical configuration steps remain largely the same across environments, the purpose and use cases of each client and system differ:

  • Client 000 – Typically used for system-level configuration and technical setup activities.
  • Productive Client – Used for business operations and application-related processes.
  • Development (DEV) System – Used for development, configuration, and testing activities.
  • Production (PROD) System – Used for live business operations.

By ensuring that the integration is properly configured across these systems and clients, SAP Cloud ALM can effectively monitor system activities, manage transports, and support lifecycle operations across the entire SAP landscape. 

Login to 000 in client /n/SDF/ALM_SETUP

Maintaining the HTTP Destination

To configure the HTTP destination, a service key from SAP Cloud ALM is required. This service key contains the necessary credentials and endpoint details that allow the on-premise system to securely communicate with the cloud environment.

The service key is generated within SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), where SAP Cloud ALM is provisioned.

Procedure

  1. Log in to the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) cockpit.
  2. Navigate to the subaccount associated with your SAP Cloud ALM tenant.
  3. Locate the SAP Cloud ALM service instance.
  4. Generate a service key, which will be used to maintain the HTTP destination in the on-premise system.

The information from the service key will later be used to configure the required communication settings between the on-premise SAP S/4HANA system and SAP Cloud ALM.

Registering the System in SAP Cloud ALM

Enter the background user that was created to perform the data collection tasks. Ensure that this user has the required authorizations as described in the Prerequisites section.

Next, choose Register to initiate the connection with SAP Cloud ALM and register the system. If the registration is successful, an LMS ID from the Landscape Management Service is automatically retrieved and displayed. This ID confirms that the system has been successfully registered in SAP Cloud ALM.

  1. Activating Transport Management Use Cases in SAP Cloud ALM

After registering the system using transaction /SDF/ALM_SETUP, the next step is to activate the required transport-related use cases in SAP Cloud ALM.
The tasks to be activated depend on the system landscape (DEV, QUA, PRD) and the client in which the configuration is performed.

  1. Development System (DEV)

Client 000: Activate the following task:

Transports: Read; This task must be activated on source systems to report transport requests to SAP Cloud ALM so that they can be assigned to features.

Client 100 (Development Productive Client)

Activate: Transports: Create & Export (Client-Specific)

This task must be activated on all development clients used for customizing activities.

For example:

  • If customizing activities are performed in client 100, activate the task there.
  • If another client such as 200 is used for development or transport creation, the task must also be activated in that client.

Activating this task triggers the background job:

/SDF/CALM_CDM_TR_PROC_CL_DEP-XXX

This enables the following transport-related functions:

  • Create transports
  • Create transport of copies
  • Release transports
  • Delete empty transports during release

Transports: Retrofit (Client-Specific)

Activate this task in the development system of your maintenance track if retrofit functionality is required.

Retrofit ensures that changes implemented in one landscape can be synchronized with another development track.

  1. Quality System (QUA)

Client 000; Activate the following task:

Transports: Import

This triggers the background job:

/SDF/CALM_CDM_IMPORT_TRANSPORTS

The job performs the following actions:

  • Retrieves transports scheduled for import from SAP Cloud ALM
  • Executes the transport import process into the target system

Target systems typically include:

  • Quality Assurance (QA)
  • Pre-Production
  • Production

These systems are assigned to the project via the deployment plan.

  1. Production System (PRD)

Client 000; Activate the following tasks:

Transports: Import

Allows SAP Cloud ALM to trigger transport imports into the production system.

Transports: Read Landscape

This task reports the STMS landscape configuration to SAP Cloud ALM.

Supported landscape configurations include:

  • Any consistent Transport Management System (TMS) landscape
  • The last system in a transport track is always considered the production system
  • Client-specific transport routes (CTC) are recommended
  • TMS transport groups are supported

Transports: Check; This feature enables transport impact analysis before importing transports into production.

It evaluates the transport set to identify potential risks or conflicts that may impact the production environment.

Do the same for 000 and productive client of production system as well.

  1. Background Jobs Created After SAP Cloud ALM Configuration

Once the configuration and system registration are completed in SAP Cloud ALM, several background jobs are automatically scheduled in the SAP S/4HANA (or ABAP) system. These jobs are responsible for data collection, system monitoring, transport processing, and communication with SAP Cloud ALM.

Some of the commonly scheduled jobs include:

  • /SDF/CALM_CDM_DIAGNOSTICS_000
  • /SDF/CALM_CDM_DIAGNOSTICS_<Productive Client>
  • /SDF/CALM_CDM_TR_CHECK
  • /SDF/CALM_CDM_TR_PROC_CL_DEP-<Client No.>

In addition, the following operational jobs may also be scheduled:

  • CALM Auto Discovery – RFCALMPI1<Client No.> / ZCALM_PI1_00
  • CALM Heartbeat – RFCALMPI1<Client No.> / ZCALM_PI1_000
  • CALM Scheduler – RFCALMPI1<Client No.> / ZCALM_PI1_000

These jobs ensure continuous communication, monitoring, and transport management synchronization between the on-premise system and SAP Cloud ALM.

Now go to projects and setup and add the system.

Add the systems to SAP Cloud ALM based on the number of systems available in your landscape. In this example, the environment consists of two landscapes: Development and Production. These systems are added and organized accordingly to enable proper monitoring, transport management, and lifecycle operations within SAP Cloud ALM.

  1. Creating Deployment Plans

The next step is to create deployment plans in SAP Cloud ALM. Deployment plans define how transports move across the system landscape and support different change management scenarios.

These plans may vary depending on organizational requirements and client-specific processes.

Common examples include:

  • Normal Change
  • Standard Change etc.

Create the required deployment plans based on your organization’s change management process. After creating the plans, assign the system groups that were configured in the earlier steps. This ensures that transports are deployed according to the defined system landscape and transport routes.

By configuring deployment plans properly, SAP Cloud ALM can effectively manage and control the movement of transports across development, testing, and production environments.

In the next step, assign the required roles for Change and Deployment Management in SAP Cloud ALM. These roles define the responsibilities of users involved in the change management process.

Typical roles include Project Lead, Change Manager, Developer, and other relevant stakeholders. Assigning these roles ensures proper governance, clear accountability, and smooth execution of the change and transport management process.

With these steps completed, the configuration and integration with SAP Cloud ALM are successfully finalized. The on-premise systems are now connected, enabling centralized monitoring, transport management, and change and deployment management across the system landscape

  1. System Refresh Considerations

After connecting your ABAP system to SAP Cloud ALM, the system continuously sends monitoring data to the SAP Cloud ALM monitoring applications.

The monitoring data is associated with a specific ABAP system in the Landscape Management application based on the Landscape Management System (LMS) ID.

When a system refresh is performed—where an ABAP system is overwritten with a copy of another system—the original LMS ID mapping may become inconsistent. As a result, monitoring data may incorrectly be assigned to the source system of the copy rather than to the refreshed system.

To prevent this issue, it is recommended to save and restore the tables containing SAP Cloud ALM monitoring configuration during the refresh process.

Post-Copy Automation (PCA) Integration

The SAP Cloud ALM configuration is integrated with Post-Copy Automation (PCA) to simplify system copy and refresh scenarios.

Prerequisites

Ensure the following components are available:

  • ST-PI 7.40 SP27 or higher
  • Implementation of SAP Note 3501074 (System Copy: ST-PI CALM Configuration)

Reports for System Copy and Refresh

If Post-Copy Automation (PCA) is not used, SAP provides dedicated reports to support system copy and refresh scenarios related to SAP Cloud ALM configuration.

Report: /SDF/CALM_SYS_COPY

This report is used after performing a system copy to create a new system based on an already configured ABAP system.

The report deletes the existing SAP Cloud ALM configuration associated with the copied system to prevent incorrect system mapping.

After running this report, the connection to SAP Cloud ALM must be re-established using transaction:

/SDF/ALM_SETUP

To prepare a system refresh, you can execute this report. It creates a Transport of Copies (ToC) with the table content of the SAP Cloud ALM configuration. Export this request and save it. After the system refresh, you can import this transport request again.

entry.title: Customizing GRC NWBC screens globally without PFCG role update
entry.content:
Challenge: Fragmented GRC NWBC appearance and work center structure for different user groups in organisation leading to inconsistent UI layouts when accessing GRC functionality through NWBC, leading to reduced productivity and increased training costs. A uniform NWBC Landing page need to be established.

 

Implementation Approach: A uniform NWBC Landing page need to be configured which is applicable across all users accessing NWBC launch screen without changing roles.

In this blog, the detailed steps for configuring NWBC screens in highlighted.

Benefits

  • Eliminates Need for Technical ABAP Changes

Many minor UI adjustments, such as showing/hiding: Hyperlinks, Menu sections, Application nodes can be handled via customizing mode without involving developers

  • Helps Maintain Audit & Compliance Standards

Since NWBC is used by auditors, risk teams, and provisioning teams, controlling UI visibility helps ensure Least privilege navigation, No exposure of admin tools to business users, Clear segmentation of duties within the UI.

 

Environment: GRC Access Control 12.0

 

To achieve Centralized Control of UI Structure, GRC Admins can change what appears on NWBC for all users, from a single configuration point. No need to adjust individual roles repeatedly.

 In NWBC landing page, Right click -> option ‘Call Application in customizing mode’.

 

Segregation of Duties approach to prevent unintended customization:

Authorization object S_WDR_ADM controls the access to option ‘Call Application in customizing mode’ through which elements like visibility of each hyperlink/section can be globally changed for all users.

  • GRC Admins (Super user) or User designated to perform NWBC UI changes can be assigned with Authorization object S_WDR_ADM with full access in the PFCG role
  • For all other users, restrict Authorization object S_WDR_ADM to display only or disable it in the PFCG role.

 

Steps:

NOTE:  Exercise caution in applying these changes as it will be applicable to all users accessing NWBC irrespective of role assignment.

For Example - If there is a requirement that hyperlink ‘Work Inbox – Simplified’ should not be visible to any users who uses NWBC, then we can follow the below steps

1. Right Click on hyperlink ‘Work Inbox – Simplified’ -> Call Application in Customizing mode

2. Choose the Transport Request

3. Right click -> Call Application in Classic Customizing Mode

4. Right click -> Settings for Current Configuration

5. Set the Visibility to Invisible. 

6. After the changes are applied, ‘Work Inbox – Simplified’ hyperlink is not visible. You can always switch it back to default by following the same previous steps

 

You can follow the same steps to customise other NWBC screen hyperlinks or buttons.

 

Conclusion:

You have now successfully customised the GRC NWBC screen to display only required hyperlinks for all users.

entry.title: Configuring custom GRC NWBC Launch Screens restricted via PFCG roles
entry.content:
Challenge: Standard SAP GRC roles for NetWeaver Business Client (NWBC) grant excessive access privileges that exceed users' actual job requirements. This over-provisioning creates security risks and poor user experience, as users encounter numerous irrelevant hyperlinks and folders they don't need for their daily tasks.

Implementation Approach: The solution leverages transaction LPD_CUST (Configure Launchpad for Menus) to design custom NWBC landing pages. This involves copying standard Webdynpro application configuration, component configuration and UIBB component in which LPD_CUST role is linked.

In this blog, the detailed steps for configuring LPD_CUST, Web Dynpro Configuration and PFCG mapping is highlighted.

Environment: GRC Access Control 12.0

Launchpad Flow chart:

 

Section 1: LPD_CUST launchpad customisation

In Tcode LPD_CUST, we need to define the customization that is required for the workset, here we have considered GRACHOME (GRC Access Control Home Workset). It is recommended to take a copy of this workset to custom naming convention to preserve standard configuration.

Select line item GRACHOME, Click on copy.

 

 

Enter your custom values and namespace.

 

 

In ZGRACHOME, You can make all the required customisations. Here, for simplicity, I have modified the folder name to ‘Work Inbox - CUSTOM’.

Potential Use case 1: For Approvers users, you can keep only ‘Work Inbox’, Delete ‘Work Inbox -  Simplified’ to make it more clear to users as to which one to use.

Potential Use case 2: In Application help, You can place company’s intranet URL link that points to a guide on how to review and approve requests. For this, you can create ‘New Application’ , select application type ‘URL’ and add the required URL.

 

You can follow the same steps to customize Setup, Access Management, Reports and Analytics Webdynpro Application

 

Section 2: NWBC Webdynpro customisation

In tcode PFCG, copy standard role SAP_GRAC_NWBC to custom role Z_TEST_NWBC,

Tcode PFCG -> Z_TEST_NWBC

Here, we are customizing the ‘My Home’ Webdynpro application GRFN_SERVICE_MAP.

Right click on ‘My Home’ Webdynpro application -> Details

We see the standard Configuration: GRAC_FPM_AC_LPD_HOME. We want to customize this Application Configuration to point our desired ‘My Home’ Layout in NWBC which we configured in LPD_CUST.

Click on ‘Application Configuration’ button.

 

This will launch a web URL which takes you to application configuration.

 

 

From here on, the logic is that we copy the Application Configuration, Component Configuration, UIBB Configuration and In UIBB configuration we point it to Role /instance of LPD_CUST. Refer to the flowchart.

Step 1: Create Copy of Application Configuration.

 
Click on Other Functions -> New Window -> Copy

 

 

 

Provide custom configuration ID and description

 

Select the relevant package, here I chose $TMP (Non-transportable), Click OK.

 

 

Click Change

 

We enter into the custom application configuration Z1GRAC_FPM_AC_LPD_HOME

Click on hyper link GRAC_FPM_CC_LPD_HOME

 

You will reach Component Configuration screen as below

 

Step 2: Create copy of component configuration

Click on Other Functions -> New Window -> Copy

 

Provide the custom configuration ID and description

 

Enter the desired package

 

Click Change

 

Click on Edit -> Cancel to get in Display mode, Click on hyperlink ‘GRAC_FPM_UIBB_LPD_HOME’

 

Step 3: Create copy of UIBB configuration

In this window, Click on Other Functions -> New Window

Click Copy

 

Provide the custom configuration ID and description

 

Click Change.

In this window, we need to map ZGRACHOME in Role field (earlier created in LPD_CUST).

Change it to ZGRACHOME. All the customizations done in ZGRACHOME can be previewed. Notice that we had changed folder ‘Work Inbox – CUSTOM’ in this scenario.

Click Save.

Select the TR and Click OK

 

Section 3: PFCG Role Configuration mapping

Step 1: In tcode PFCG, Role Z_TEST_NWBC

Right click on ‘My Home’ -> Details -> Change configuration to Z1GRAC_FPM_AC_LPD_HOME

Click Save

 

Step 2: Again, Right click on ‘My Home’ -> Details -> Click Application Configuration.

 

This will open up URL in web browser, we see that we are in custom application configuration Z1GRAC_FPM_AC_LPD_HOME, we see standard component configuration is mapped, we will need to change to custom component configuration Z1GRAC_FPM_CC_LPD_HOME

 

Click on Edit -> Assign Configuration Name

Assign the custom component configuration ID Z1GRAC_FPM_CC_LPD_HOME

Click Save.

 

Step 3: Click the hyperlink Z1GRAC_FPM_CC_LPD_HOME

 

This will open up Custom component configuration, we see that standard UIBB is mapped, but we need to change to custom UIBB Z1GRAC_FPM_UIBB_LPD_HOME

 

Click on F4 button on GRAC_FPM_UIBB_LPD_HOME, change it to Z1GRAC_FPM_UIBB_LPD_HOME

Click Save

 

Step 4: Click on Edit -> Cancel. We are in display mode. Click on hyperlink Z1GRAC_FPM_UIBB_LPD_HOME

 

You will find all the elements mapped as per the flowchart

 

Assign the PFCG role Z_TEST_NWBC to user and test the changes. Or you can Right click on ‘My Home’ -> Execute.

 

The customisation done in LPD_CUST reflects. 

NOTE: If you assign standard role SAP_GRAC_NWBC and custom role Z_TEST_NWBC together to a user, this won’t work as intended since the system is confused whether to pick up the standard application configuration or the custom application configuration defined in the Webdynpro application GRFN_SERVICE_MAP. The intent of creating the custom role is to assign that specific custom role Z_TEST_NWBC to satisfy all NWBC access requirements for the user.

You can follow the same steps to customize Setup, Access Management, Reports and Analytics Webdynpro Application.

 

Conclusion:

You have now successfully created a custom PFCG role with Web Dynpro application configuration pointing to Custom NWBC screen.

entry.title: SAP UX Update: The New Joule Work Engagement Layer Experiences
entry.content:
What is Joule Work, and what does it mean for you? What do the new Joule Work engagement layer experiences mean for existing application experiences? How can you try it out? Read on to get an overview of Joule Work and answers to these questions.

What is Joule Work?

Joule Work provides an AI-based user experience that transforms how work happens. With Joule work, people express what they want to accomplish, and Joule surfaces the right insights, automates routine work, and coordinates AI agents across systems to achieve it – all in one dynamic workspace. It acts as an engagement layer that spans across SAP products and beyond, and coexists with current experiences provided by pre-defined application UIs.

It represents a new way of interacting with business systems, since it provides:

  • One single place where a user can get tasks done, rather than the traditional app-centric interaction model, where users need to navigate to different applications or even to different products.
  • An intent-driven, situational experience, where users state their intents in natural language and the system builds on the fly accordingly, taking the current business and user context (the current situation) into account: fetching data, triggering AI agents, and visualizing the outcome appropriately.

Figure 1: Joule Work experiences: Conversations, Spaces, and Develop. Alt Text: The image shows 3 screenshots, one each for the three experiences. The top of the image has a heading  “Joule Work”, above a row with five attributes: Dynamic, Proactive, Agentic, Intent-based, Omnipresent.

Joule Work provides these experiences:

  • Conversations: this experience allows people to express intent and explore what they want to accomplish through dialogue with Joule. It is an AI-powered chat supporting role-based intents, multiple conversation threads, with type-ahead, auto-suggestions and multi-facetted types of responses (text, tables, charts etc.). Joule brings together guidance and outcomes from across SAP and third-party systems. Conversations can also act as an entry point to Spaces for more complex workflows.
  • Spaces: provides flexible AI-generated spaces that dynamically adapt content based on user intent and context, for dealing with simple as well as complex tasks and issues. Each space is composed in real time to bring the right data, tools, and context together for a specific goal, allowing people to work in an environment that adapts to their present goal, instead of navigating applications and their pre-defined UIs.​
  • Develop: this experience is provided by Joule Studio, enabling developers to vibe-code, test and deploy AI agents, applications, and automations across SAP and third-party systems. Joule Work provides a convenient entry point to Joule Studio for developers and business users alike, giving them an AI-powered user experience for pro-code and low-code tools.

To get a better idea of these experiences, have a look at Joule Work in action:

Click on the image to start the video.

Joule Work mobile app

The Joule Work mobile app provides Joule Work Conversations and Spaces for mobile phones and tablets. Figure 2 shows some examples.

Figure 2: Joule Work mobile app, examples of Conversations and Spaces. Alt Text: two pairs of iPhone images. On the left Joule Work Conversations, showing a start and a screen listing some return orders and a user prompt. On the right a screen with text “Create a new space” and a user input “Create a space to sow my open customer returns for BikePro”, and a screen showing the resulting space with a chart, some cards with recommended tasks, and a return order analysis.

Find out more here. 

  • Joule Work mobile app is here!

What are the benefits for businesses and their users?

Businesses expect that the new AI/agentic, intent-driven way of working that is taking hold across the industry is ushering in a new era of user interaction and user experience, that will bring them gains in productivity and data quality, along with better business insights and outcomes, while saving training costs and reducing user-support costs.

Joule Work from SAP has an ingrained understanding of the customer’s business processes: hence it is best placed to deliver on the above promise. It can provide experiences that bring together the right data and services from across our applications to guide users from initial intent to a successful outcome.

It helps users get their work done faster, more easily, and with better outcomes, by:

  • Providing one place to get their work done, so they don’t have to navigate to many different applications and products, and don’t have to navigate through layers of application UIs, thus reducing the number of clicks and interactions significantly.
  • Allowing users to state their intent in natural language with a conversational interface, then triggering the relevant actions to achieve their intent, and showing the outcome(s) in a personalized way, tailored to the user’s needs.

Which target groups of users and use cases is Joule Work meant for?

Business and technical users. The goal is to help all our customer’s SAP users get their work done more easily, focusing on outcomes, so it is not limited to specific target groups.

The Joule Work Develop experience is provided by Joule Studio. Find out more about the new version of Joule Studio in this news article:

  • Announcing New Joule Studio for Enterprise Scale Agentic Development.

What does this mean for existing UIs and SAP Fiori apps?

Existing SAP products and applications built with SAP’s UI technology stack, as well as other apps in our portfolio, will continue to progress with their roadmap and planned product strategy. Joule Work has no impact on existing UIs, instead it complements them. Customers will continue to get the benefits from existing applications and their investment is protected.

Joule Work provides an additional option for users to interact with the system. Wherever they find that it helps them get tasks done more easily and/or with better outcomes, they are likely to do those tasks with Joule Work. Wherever they feel that their familiar, pre-defined applications are better suited to a task, they will continue using them.

This means that SAP delivers two kinds of experiences:

  • Application experiences (from SAP Cloud ERP, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Analytics Cloud, etc.): SAP and customer-built transactional and analytical application experiences.
  • Engagement layer experiences (Joule Work experiences): AI-native, agentic, and generative experiences that help users interact with and orchestrate work across those applications.

Our users are used to application UIs and pre-defined navigation paths, how to manage the transition?

The existing product home pages and cross-product entry points such as SAP Build Work Zone and Joule Work mobile app (formerly called SAP Mobile Start) continue to be available for providing pre-defined as well as customer-defined pages and navigation menus. Similarly, the existing applications continue to be available, with their application experiences.

Users working in the web browser can open Joule Work from Joule and hence start getting familiar with the new way of working. Since the new experience co-exists with the current experience it will be easy for users to adapt and adopt. We expect the transition to be user-led, in that they will choose whichever experience helps them get specific tasks done more easily and/or with better outcomes.

If users are already familiar with popular consumer applications such as ChatGPT, Google Gemini or Claude Sonnet, then they will be able to build on this to leverage the next generation user experience provided by Joule Work, with the new ways of interacting.

Does it complement or does it replace existing solutions such as SAP Start, SAP Build Work Zone, and SAP Mobile Start?

It complements them. The existing solutions allow customer administrators to pre-define layouts, content and navigation options, which helps users access applications and information using familiar pages, menus and links. It makes it easy for them to understand the site and feel comfortable using it in their daily work. This is something that benefits all users but is especially helpful for users new to the system to get started quickly and get familiar with their work environment.

SAP Mobile Start has been rebranded to Joule Work mobile app, accompanied by a new app icon and an updated user experience. For customers previously accessing Joule within SAP Mobile Start, the Joule entry point has transitioned from the diamond in the header bar to a full-screen interface to be accessed via the tab bar. All core functionality and features remain unchanged, ensuring continuity for both customers and end users with no disruption to existing workflows. In addition to the core functionality, it provides the Joule Work engagement layer experience for mobile devices. Find out more here:

  • SAP Mobile Start - Rebranding of the app, enhanced Joule and improved single sign-on functionality.

For SAP Cloud ERP Private: would we need to go to SAP Fiori (implement SAP Fiori launchpad), or can we skip it and go to Joule Work directly?

You would need to implement the SAP Fiori launchpad for Joule Work to work with SAP Cloud ERP Private: they use the navigation and authorization information defined by customers in the SAP Fiori launchpad to access the system. Also, Joule only runs in the browser on desktops and is launched from the launchpad. Similarly, if users navigate from Joule Work to applications, this navigation will be to browser-based applications running in the launchpad.

How can partners and customers extend standard SAP functionality for Joule Work – as a key user or as a developer?

The established programming model for creating extensions continues to be valid. Joule Work accesses OData services and their meta-data (amongst others) and hence will also access extensions or custom OData services that have been developed according to the SAP BTP Developer’s Guide.

Using Joule Studio or SAP Build is the way to build extensions and agents, for Joule Work as well as for pre-defined applications.

If you are building new applications, follow the SAP BTP Developer’s Guide, and use Joule Studio or SAP Build. You can build agents on top of the APIs you create, and Joule Work will access the OData services and meta-data of your new applications. The Fiori apps you create using SAPUI5 / SAP Fiori elements will allow users to use your new functionality using apps with pre-defined UIs, an experience that they are familiar with. Also, users can use these UIs to confirm that what was done by Joule Work is indeed correct and hence build trust in it.

What is available today (Sapphire 2026)

As described above, this first version of Joule Work provides Conversations, Spaces, and Develop, with the Joule Work Develop experience being provided by Joule Studio. The Sapphire scope:

  • Supports integration to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and SAP SuccessFactors.
  • Supports exactly one SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you cannot connect to more than one.
  • Does not support document grounding in Joule.

How can customers get Joule Work, and what are the pre-requisites?

If you are interested, you can sign up to the limited Early Adopter Care (EAC) program. The pre-requisites for this at the time of writing are as follows:

  • Joule needs to be SAP-managed.
    • New SAP-managed Joule instances are supported
    • Existing customers with customer-managed Joule would need to transition to SAP-managed Joule.
  • Available only in specific regions: USA and EU.

Final words

I hope you are as excited as I am about the huge potential these Joule Work experiences have for helping our users get their work done faster and more easily, with better outcomes. It will be interesting to observe how people in various roles adopt this new way of working, and how fast they adopt: for which tasks do users prefer to use Joule Work, for which ones do they continue to prefer application experiences – and why?

If you want to dig a bit deeper, have a look at how we are designing these new experiences, and see how we are evolving the SAP Design System for AI-driven UX:

  • Designing the Autonomous Enterprise.
  • The Compositional Design System.
  • AI and Design.

If you want an overview of interesting UX shown at Sapphire in Orlando this week, have a look at  Peter Spielvogel's nice summary:

  • Sapphire 2026. The future of UX is here. Are you ready for an AI -powered world?

entry.title: Test Migration Guide for Adapt Filters Dialog within the FilterBar
entry.content:
Overview

If you're maintaining OPA tests for applications using the Adapt Filters dialog, you'll need to update your test code to work with the revised UI structure. This guide walks you through the key changes and shows you exactly how to adapt your tests.

Prerequisites

To use the revised Adapt Filters dialog, you need to upgrade to SAPUI5 version 1.146 or a higher version. The current version is SAPUI5 1.148.

Understanding the Fundamental Changes

The revised version of the Adapt Filters dialog introduces a mode-based interaction model that separates different operations. Instead of having all functionality available in one place, the dialog now uses dedicated modes for different tasks:

  • Edit mode is used for adding filters, removing filters, and toggling visibility
  • Sort mode is used exclusively for reordering filters

This separation prevents accidental changes and makes each operation more intentional.

Key UI Changes at a Glance

The visual structure has changed significantly:

List/Group View Toggle: The icon-based toggle buttons have been replaced with text-based tabs. Instead of clicking on sap-icon://list or sap-icon://group-2, users now click on labeled "List" or "Group" tabs.

Filter Items: The old sap.m.ColumnListItem with checkboxes has been replaced with sap.m.CustomListItem, containing action buttons and filter controls.

Adding Filters: Instead of checking a checkbox in a long list of all available filters, users now select from a ComboBox dropdown labeled "Add Filter" at the bottom of the panel.

Removing Filters: Rather than unchecking a checkbox, users click an X button (with remove icon) in Edit mode.

Visibility Control: Filters can be hidden. An eye icon (with or without strikethrough) toggles visibility.

Reordering: The dedicated reorder buttons (Move Up, Down, Top, Bottom) are gone. Instead, users drag and drop filters in Sort mode. As an alternative, users can also reorder filter items with keyboard shortcuts as described under "Keyboard Shortcuts for Reordering".

Changed: The view toggle moved from icon buttons to text-based tabs.

Filter Button (New Feature): A new "Filter" button has been added to the dialog. When clicked, the dialog closes, and the filter bar control applies the current filter values to the associated control (Table, Chart, etc.). This provides a direct way to filter without having to close the dialog separately and then trigger filtering manually.

Test Code Examples

The following sections show what your test code could look like for individual actions. You can combine these examples to create complete test workflows.

Please note: In the code samples, globals are used, for example new sap.ui.test.actions.Press()

This explanation has been included in the code sample for clarity. Please do not use globals.

Import the dependencies via sap.ui.define and use new Press() in your test code instead.

Opening the Adapt Filters Dialog

To open the Adapt Filters dialog, your test code could look like this:

this.waitFor({
    controlType: "sap.m.Button",
    matchers: new sap.ui.test.matchers.Properties({
        text: "Adapt Filters"
    }),
    actions: new sap.ui.test.actions.Press(),
    success: function() {
        Opa5.assert.ok(true, "Adapt Filters Dialog opened");
    }
});

Closing the Adapt Filters Dialog

To close the dialog, your test code could look like this:

this.waitFor({
    searchOpenDialogs: true,
    controlType: "sap.m.Button",
    matchers: new sap.ui.test.matchers.Properties({
        text: "OK"
    }),
    actions: new sap.ui.test.actions.Press(),
    success: function() {
        Opa5.assert.ok(true, "Adapt Filters Dialog closed");
    }
});

Switching to List View

To switch to List view, your test code could look like this:

this.waitFor({
    searchOpenDialogs: true,
    controlType: "sap.m.IconTabFilter",
    matchers: new sap.ui.test.matchers.Properties({
        text: "List"
    }),
    actions: new sap.ui.test.actions.Press(),
    success: function() {
        Opa5.assert.ok(true, "Switched to List view");
    }
});

Switching to Group View

To switch to Group view, your test code could look like this:

this.waitFor({
    searchOpenDialogs: true,
    controlType: "sap.m.IconTabFilter",
    matchers: new sap.ui.test.matchers.Properties({
        text: "Group"
    }),
    actions: new sap.ui.test.actions.Press(),
    success: function() {
        Opa5.assert.ok(true, "Switched to Group view");
    }
});

Switching to Edit Mode

To switch to Edit mode, your test code could look like this:

this.waitFor({
    searchOpenDialogs: true,
    controlType: "sap.m.Button",
    matchers: new sap.ui.test.matchers.PropertyStrictEquals({
        name: "text",
        value: "Edit"
    }),
    actions: new sap.ui.test.actions.Press(),
    success: function() {
        Opa5.assert.ok(true, "Switched to Edit mode");
    }
});

Switching to Sort Mode

To switch to Sort mode, your test code could look like this:

this.waitFor({
    searchOpenDialogs: true,
    controlType: "sap.m.Button",
    matchers: new sap.ui.test.matchers.PropertyStrictEquals({
        name: "text",
        value: "Sort"
    }),
    actions: new sap.ui.test.actions.Press(),
    success: function() {
        Opa5.assert.ok(true, "Switched to Sort mode");
    }
});

Adding a Filter

To add a filter via the ComboBox, your test code could look like this:

this.waitFor({
    searchOpenDialogs: true,
    controlType: "sap.m.ComboBox",
    success: function(aComboBoxes) {
        const oComboBox = aComboBoxes[0];
        new sap.ui.test.actions.Press().executeOn(oComboBox);

        this.waitFor({
            searchOpenDialogs: true,
            controlType: "sap.m.StandardListItem",
            matchers: new sap.ui.test.matchers.Properties({
                title: "Country"  // Replace with your filter name
            }),
            actions: new sap.ui.test.actions.Press(),
            success: function() {
                Opa5.assert.ok(true, "Filter 'Country' added");
            }
        });
    }.bind(this)
});

Finding a Filter Item by Name

To find a specific filter item in the list, your test code could look like this:

this.waitFor({
    searchOpenDialogs: true,
    controlType: "sap.m.CustomListItem",
    matchers: function(oItem) {
        const aContent = oItem.getContent();
        if (aContent && aContent[0]) {
            const aGridContent = aContent[0].getContent();
            return aGridContent.some(function(oControl) {
                return oControl.getText && oControl.getText() === "Country:";  // Replace with your filter name
            });
        }
        return false;
    },
    success: function(aItems) {
        Opa5.assert.ok(aItems.length > 0, "Filter item 'Country' found");
        // Store aItems[0] for further actions
    }
});

Removing a Filter

Note: You must be in Edit mode and find the filter item first (see "Finding a Filter Item by Name").

To remove a filter, your test code could look like this:

this.waitFor({
    controlType: "sap.m.Button",
    matchers: [
        new sap.ui.test.matchers.Ancestor(oFilterItem),  // oFilterItem from previous step
        new sap.ui.test.matchers.Properties({
            icon: "sap-icon://decline"
        })
    ],
    actions: new sap.ui.test.actions.Press(),
    success: function() {
        Opa5.assert.ok(true, "Filter removed");
    }
});

Toggling Filter Visibility (Show/Hide)

Note: You must be in Edit mode and find the filter item first (see "Finding a Filter Item by Name").

To toggle the visibility of a filter, your test code could look like this:

this.waitFor({
    controlType: "sap.m.Button",
    matchers: [
        new sap.ui.test.matchers.Ancestor(oFilterItem),  // oFilterItem from previous step
        new sap.ui.test.matchers.Properties({
            icon: "sap-icon://show"  // or "sap-icon://hide" depending on current state
        })
    ],
    actions: new sap.ui.test.actions.Press(),
    success: function() {
        Opa5.assert.ok(true, "Filter visibility toggled");
    }
});

Important: If a filter field has no value, hiding it will cause it to be removed entirely when the panel is reopened. To keep a filter in the panel while hidden, ensure it has a value before toggling visibility to hidden.

Reordering Filters via Drag and Drop

Note: You must be in Sort mode and find both source and target filter items first.

To reorder filters, your test code could look like this:

// Drag the source filter
new sap.ui.test.actions.Drag().executeOn(oSourceFilterItem);

// Drop on the target filter
new sap.ui.test.actions.Drop({ before: true }).executeOn(oTargetFilterItem);  // Use before: false to drop after
Opa5.assert.ok(true, "Filter reordered");

Complete Workflow Examples

Now that you have the test code examples, here are complete workflow examples that combine them.

Workflow: Adding a New Filter

Steps:

  1. Open the Adapt Filters dialog
  2. Switch to List view (recommended)
  3. Add the filter using the ComboBox
  4. Close the dialog

Usage scenario: You need to add one or more filters to the filter bar.

Workflow: Removing an Existing Filter

Steps:

  1. Open the Adapt Filters dialog
  2. Switch to List view
  3. Switch to Edit mode
  4. Find the filter item by name
  5. Click the X button (decline icon) to remove it
  6. Close the dialog

Usage scenario: You need to completely remove a filter from the filter bar.

Workflow: Hiding a Filter Without Removing It

Steps:

  1. Open the Adapt Filters dialog
  2. Switch to List view
  3. Switch to Edit mode
  4. Find the filter item by name
  5. Click the eye icon to toggle visibility
  6. Close the dialog

Usage scenario: You need to temporarily hide a filter but want to keep its configuration and value. Remember: filters without values will be removed when hidden.

Workflow: Reordering Filters

Steps:

  1. Open the Adapt Filters dialog
  2. Switch to List view
  3. Switch to Sort mode
  4. Find the source filter item (the one to move)
  5. Find the target filter item (where to move it)
  6. Drag the source filter and drop it on the target
  7. Close the dialog

Usage scenario: You need to change the order in which filters appear in the filter bar.

Workflow: Switching Between List and Group Views

Steps:

  1. Open the Adapt Filters Dialog
  2. Click on the "List" or "Group" tab
  3. Perform your desired actions
  4. Close the dialog

Usage scenario: You need to work with individual filters (List view) or grouped filters (Group view).

Helper Functions

You can create reusable helper functions from the building blocks:

Helper: Add Multiple Filters

const fnAddFilter = function(sFilterName) {
    return this.waitFor({
        searchOpenDialogs: true,
        controlType: "sap.m.ComboBox",
        success: function(aComboBoxes) {
            const oComboBox = aComboBoxes[0];
            new sap.ui.test.actions.Press().executeOn(oComboBox);

            this.waitFor({
                searchOpenDialogs: true,
                controlType: "sap.m.StandardListItem",
                matchers: new sap.ui.test.matchers.Properties({
                    title: sFilterName
                }),
                actions: new sap.ui.test.actions.Press(),
                success: function() {
                    Opa5.assert.ok(true, "Filter '" + sFilterName + "' added");
                }
            });
        }.bind(this)
    });
}.bind(this);

// Usage:
fnAddFilter("Country");
fnAddFilter("Language");
fnAddFilter("Genre");

Helper: Finding and Removing Filters

const fnRemoveFilter = function(sFilterName) {
    return this.waitFor({
        searchOpenDialogs: true,
        controlType: "sap.m.CustomListItem",
        matchers: function(oItem) {
            const aContent = oItem.getContent();
            if (aContent && aContent[0]) {
                const aGridContent = aContent[0].getContent();
                return aGridContent.some(function(oControl) {
                    return oControl.getText && oControl.getText() === sFilterName + ":";
                });
            }
            return false;
        },
        success: function(aItems) {
            this.waitFor({
                controlType: "sap.m.Button",
                matchers: [
                    new sap.ui.test.matchers.Ancestor(aItems[0]),
                    new sap.ui.test.matchers.Properties({
                        icon: "sap-icon://decline"
                    })
                ],
                actions: new sap.ui.test.actions.Press(),
                success: function() {
                    Opa5.assert.ok(true, "Filter '" + sFilterName + "' removed");
                }
            });
        }.bind(this)
    });
}.bind(this);

// Usage (ensure you're in Edit mode first):
fnRemoveFilter("Country");

Keyboard Shortcuts for Reordering

The new panel also introduces keyboard shortcuts for reordering filters when in Sort mode. While these aren't directly testable via standard OPA5 (they require focus and modifier keys), you should be aware of them for manual testing:

  • CMD + Home (Mac) / Ctrl + Home (Windows): Move filter to the top
  • CMD + End (Mac) / Ctrl + End (Windows): Move filter to the bottom
  • CMD + Arrow Up (Mac) / Ctrl + Arrow Up (Windows): Move filter one position up
  • CMD + Arrow Down (Mac) / Ctrl + Arrow Down (Windows): Move filter one position down

For automated OPA tests, use the drag-and-drop approach shown above.

Quick Reference Guide

Mode-Based Actions

Remember that certain operations now require switching to specific modes:

ActionRequired ModeUI Element
Add filterNo mode required (default)ComboBox at bottom
Remove filterEdit mode requiredX button (remove icon)
Toggle visibilityEdit mode requiredEye button (show/hide icon)
Reorder filtersSort mode requiredDrag handles (:::)

Control Type Changes

Here's a quick mapping of old to new control types:

Old ControlNew ControlUsage
sap.m.CheckBoxsap.m.ComboBox + sap.m.StandardListItemAdding filters
sap.m.CheckBoxButton with sap-icon://declineRemoving filters
N/AButton with sap-icon://show/hideVisibility toggle
sap.m.ColumnListItemsap.m.CustomListItemFilter list items
Button with reorder iconsDrag-and-drop actionsReordering
Button with iconsap.m.IconTabFilterView switching

Common Matcher Pattern for Filter Items

You'll use this pattern frequently to find filter items by their label:

matchers: function(oItem) {
    const aContent = oItem.getContent();
    if (aContent && aContent[0]) {
        const aGridContent = aContent[0].getContent();
        return aGridContent.some(function(oControl) {
            return oControl.getText && oControl.getText() === "FilterName:";
        });
    }
    return false;
}

Wrapping Up

The new Adapt Filters dialog represents a significant UX improvement, but it does require updating your OPA tests. The key takeaways:

  1. Use IconTabFilter with text for view switching
  2. Use ComboBox for adding filters
  3. Switch to Edit mode before removing filters or toggling visibility
  4. Switch to Sort mode before reordering filters
  5. Use CustomListItem with custom matchers to find filters
  6. Use drag and drop for reordering instead of button clicks

entry.title: FilterBar Adapt Filters Panel: UI Update
entry.content:
A Fresh Look at the Adapt Filters Dialog

The content of the Adapt Filters dialog has been revised to offer a clearer, more structured, and accessible user experience.

Visual Comparison: Old vs. Revised Design

Old design:

Revised design:

Mode-Based Interaction

A new mode switch further improves usability. Users can toggle between an Edit mode, where they adjust filter values and visibility, and a Sort mode, where they focus on arranging the order of filters. In the Sort mode, visual cues can be used for drag-and-drop interactions.

Easy Adding of Filters

Adding new filters is now a lot more transparent. The dialog now presents a dedicated section for adding filters, showing only those that are currently hidden and optional. This way, users won't be overwhelmed by long lists, and it helps them quickly identify which filters are available. The section is straightforward, with descriptive labels and input.

 

Smart Warnings and Guidance

The dialog also provides improved guidance when handling required filters or filters with values. Clear, contextual warnings appear when users attempt to hide a required filter, or when a hidden filter already contains a value. These messages help users understand the impact of their actions immediately. For example, if a required filter with a value is hidden, the user gets an information message that the field will not show in the filter bar.

Consistent and Intuitive Actions

Each filter entry now includes clearly identifiable actions, such as toggling visibility or removing a filter. These actions are consistently placed and use intuitive icons, so users can perform changes quickly. During the sorting of filters, a dedicated indicator appears to show that the filter can be repositioned.

 

Summary

Overall, the updated Adapt Filters dialog provides an accessible user interface, clearer workflows, and more informative messages. Users can find, adjust, and manage filters more easily, while the interface actively helps them avoid mistakes and maintain control over their filtering setup.

entry.title: UI5 Web Components Angular Wrappers Have a New Home in Fundamental NGX
entry.content:
The Angular wrapper library for UI5 Web Components has moved.

@ui5/webcomponents-ngx and @ui5/theming-ngx are now deprecated.
Their successor packages ship as part of Fundamental NGX

- @fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents
- @fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents-fiori
- @fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents-ai

What you gain

The new packages integrate with Fundamental NGX's theming, content density, and i18n systems - features that previously required manual wiring or weren't available at all.

What changed

This is a breaking change. Template bindings and some enum values need updates. See the migration guide for complete details.

How to migrate

1. Install new packages, uninstall old ones
2. Update template bindings from kebab-case to camelCase ([primary-title] → [primaryTitle])
3. Update ValueState values (Error → Negative, Warning → Critical, Success → Positive)
4. Replace theming and i18n setup

Questions or issues?

File them at: https://github.com/SAP/fundamental-ngx/issues

entry.title: Migrating from @ui5/webcomponents-ngx to @fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents*
entry.content:
Effective: 2026-04-27 - @ui5/webcomponents-ngx and @ui5/theming-ngx are deprecated and no longer maintained.

Successor packages are part of the Fundamental NGX monorepo.

Package Mapping

Old package

New package

@ui5/webcomponents-ngx

@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents

@ui5/webcomponents-ngx (fiori sub-path)

@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents-fiori

@ui5/webcomponents-ngx (AI sub-path)

@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents-ai

@ui5/theming-ngx

Remove - replaced by Fundamental NGX theming (see below)

Breaking Changes

1. Property Binding Names (kebab-case → camelCase)

All input property bindings changed from kebab-case to camelCase.

The old @ui5/webcomponents-ngx library bound HTML attributes using kebab-case. The new @fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents library exposes proper Angular input signals with camelCase names.

Every template binding must be updated

Old (@ui5/webcomponents-ngx)

New (@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents)

[primary-title]="'Title'"

[primaryTitle]="'Title'"

[value-state]="'Error'"

[valueState]="'Negative'"

[accessible-name]="'Search'"

[accessibleName]="'Search'"

[accessible-name-ref]="'labelId'"

[accessibleNameRef]="'labelId'"

[no-typeahead]="true"

[noTypeahead]="true"

[show-notifications]="true"

[showNotifications]="true"

[no-data-text]="'No results'"

[noDataText]="'No results'"

Rule: Convert every kebab-case attribute to camelCase Angular property binding. Standard HTML attributes (`id`, `class`, `style`, `slot`) are unchanged.

2. ValueState Enum Values

@ui5/webcomponents-ngx exposed ValueState using outdated property names. The new @fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents library uses the current UI5 Web Components naming:

Old (@ui5/webcomponents-ngx)

New (@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents)

Error

Negative

Warning

Critical

Success

Positive

Information

Information (unchanged)

None

None (unchanged)

Before:

<ui5-input [value-state]="'Error'"></ui5-input>
<ui5-input [value-state]="'Warning'"></ui5-input>
<ui5-input [value-state]="'Success'"></ui5-input>

After:

<ui5-input [valueState]="'Negative'"></ui5-input>
<ui5-input [valueState]="'Critical'"></ui5-input>
<ui5-input [valueState]="'Positive'"></ui5-input>

3. i18n Translation Management

@ui5/webcomponents-ngx/i18n provided built-in translation strings. The new wrappers do not include translation strings - only language code synchronization.

You must provide your own translation layer (custom I18nService, @ngx-translate, or similar).

Import Path Rewrites

Before:

// app.module.ts / app.config.ts
import { Ui5WebcomponentsModule } from '@ui5/webcomponents-ngx';
// or individual sub-paths:
import { ... } from '@ui5/webcomponents-ngx/main';
import { ... } from '@ui5/webcomponents-ngx/fiori';
import { ... } from '@ui5/webcomponents-ngx/ai';
// theming bootstrap (old)
import { Ui5ThemingModule } from '@ui5/theming-ngx';

bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, {
  providers: [
    importProvidersFrom(
      Ui5ThemingModule.forRoot({ defaultTheme: 'sap_horizon' })
    ),
  ],
});

After:

// app.config.ts — component imports are per-package now
import { Button }    from '@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents/button';
import { ShellBar }  from '@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents-fiori/shell-bar';
import { AIButton }  from '@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents-ai/ai-button';
// Theming setup — choose one approach
import { ThemingModule } from '@fundamental-ngx/core/theming';
import { provideUi5LanguageBridge } from '@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents-base/i18n';

// OPTION A: Automatic theme sync (standalone app)
import { provideUi5ThemingBridge } from '@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents-base/theming-bridge';
import { provideEnvironmentInitializer } from '@angular/core';
import { Ui5WebcomponentsMainThemingService }  from '@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents/theming';
import { Ui5WebcomponentsFioriThemingService } from '@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents-fiori/theming';

bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, {
  providers: [
    provideTheming({ defaultTheme: 'sap_horizon' }),
    provideUi5ThemingBridge(),   // listens to ThemingService and syncs automatically
    provideUi5LanguageBridge(),  // syncs language code (e.g. 'en', 'de') to UI5 Web Components
    provideEnvironmentInitializer(() => inject(Ui5WebcomponentsMainThemingService)),
    provideEnvironmentInitializer(() => inject(Ui5WebcomponentsFioriThemingService)),
  ],
});

// OPTION B: Manual theme sync (module-based app or single theme switcher)
@NgModule({
  imports: [
    ThemingModule.withConfig({ defaultTheme: 'sap_horizon' }),
    // ... UI5 component imports
  ],
  providers: [
    provideUi5LanguageBridge()  // language sync only
  ]
})
export class AppModule {}

// Then call ThemingService.setTheme() directly when switching themes:
// this.themingService.setTheme('sap_horizon_dark');

Which approach to use:

  • Option A (automatic): If your app calls `ThemingService.setTheme()` from multiple places, the bridge keeps UI5 components in sync automatically.
  • Option B (manual): If you have a single theme switcher component, calling `ThemingService.setTheme()` directly is simpler. The service loads the correct CSS; UI5 components pick it up.
  • i18n caveat: provideUi5LanguageBridge() syncs the language code (e.g. `en`, `de`, `bg`) to UI5 Web Components. It does NOT provide translation strings for your app. You must manage translations separately via a custom I18nService, @ngx-translate, or similar.

Worked example — single component

Before:

// my-app/src/app/app.component.ts
import { Component } from '@angular/core';
import { Button } from '@ui5/webcomponents-ngx/main';

@Component({
  standalone: true,
  imports: [Button],
  template: `<ui5-button design="Emphasized" accessible-name="Save button">Save</ui5-button>`,
})
export class AppComponent {}

After:

// my-app/src/app/app.component.ts
import { Component } from '@angular/core';
import { Button } from '@fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents/button';  // ← import path change

@Component({
  standalone: true,
  imports: [Button],
  template: `<ui5-button design="Emphasized" [accessibleName]="'Save button'">Save</ui5-button>`,  // ← camelCase binding
})
export class AppComponent {}

NGX APIs Now Available

Consumers of the new packages automatically gain access to Fundamental NGX cross-cutting features that were not available in the standalone @ui5/webcomponents-ngx library:

Feature

How it works

Theming

Use ThemingModule.withConfig() or provideTheming() for the initial theme. Optionally use provideUi5ThemingBridge() to auto-sync theme changes. See theming section above for both approaches.

Content density

ContentDensityObserver is injected into every wrapper component. Compact/cozy mode follows the NGX content density setting.

i18n / language

provideUi5LanguageBridge() syncs the active NGX language code to UI5 Web Components. Translation strings must be provided separately.

Steps Summary

1. Install new packages:

npm install @fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents \
               @fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents-fiori \
               @fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents-ai \
               @fundamental-ngx/ui5-webcomponents-base

2. Uninstall old packages:

npm uninstall @ui5/webcomponents-ngx @ui5/theming-ngx

3. Update template bindings - convert kebab-case to camelCase (see Breaking Changes above):

  • [primary-title] → [primaryTitle]
  • [value-state] → [valueState]
  • [accessible-name] → [accessibleName]
  • etc.

4. Update ValueState enum values:

  • Error → Negative
  • Warning → Critical
  • Success → Positive

5. Replace theming bootstrap (see Import Path Rewrites → Theming above).

6. Provide translation strings if your app used `@ui5/webcomponents-ngx/i18n` — the new wrappers only sync language codes, not translations.

7. Build and verify. No template or input/output changes required.

Migration Tool

The `@fundamental-ngx/mcp` server includes a detailed migration guide. If you have the MCP server connected, run:

get_usage_guide("migrate-from-ui5-webcomponents-ngx")

Out of Scope

This guide covers migrating between the two wrapper libraries only.

Questions and Follow-up Issues

File issues at: https://github.com/SAP/fundamental-ngx/issues
